Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control

A Theoretical Foundations and Practical Systems Manual for High-Velocity Public Health Modeling

National Data Management Center for Health (NDMC) | EPHI

2026-07-01

Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility

The Crisis of Ephemeral Environments

In computational epidemiology and public health modeling, an un-versioned environment is a non-reproducible environment. Computational outcomes depend on a complex stack of dependencies:

\[\text{Model Output} = f(\text{Code}, \text{Data}, \text{Interpreter Runtime}, \text{Transitive Dependencies}, \text{OS Kernel})\]

If any layer varies across machines, execution becomes non-deterministic. This produces the “It Works on My Machine” phenomenon, which damages the credibility of policy recommendations.

The Hierarchy of Operational Breakdown

  1. API Drift: Upstream packages deprecate functions or alter parameter syntax (e.g., a function arguments layout changes between package versions).
  2. Transitive Collision: Package A requires Package C at version 1.0; Package B requires Package C at version 2.0. Without an isolated lockfile engine, the global package manager resolves this via arbitrary mutation.
  3. Floating States: Using unconstrained packages implicitly installs different builds on different dates, creating non-deterministic bug behavior across your team.

The Core Mechanisms of Dependency Resolvers

To resolve dependency trees, modern package managers convert explicit and transitive constraints into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). They then use mathematical constraint-satisfaction algorithms to calculate a valid state across all requirements.

The Resolution Strategy

  • Topological Sorting: Dependency engines crawl through the graph to arrange steps so that every prerequisite library executes before the package that relies on it.
  • SAT Solvers: Modern package engines convert version boundaries (e.g., pandas>=2.0,<3.0) into boolean satisfiability constraints. They analyze these rules to find a single, consistent set of packages that satisfies every requirement simultaneously.
  • Lockfile Determinism: Once a valid graph is resolved, the tool serializes the configuration by saving exact version cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) to disk. This bypasses the search algorithm on subsequent installs, guaranteeing identical environment construction across all analyst workstations.

Module 2: Python Environment Determinism via uv

Architectural Blueprint and Theory of uv

Traditional Python packaging systems (pip paired with basic virtualenv) lack built-in graph locking mechanics, leading to fragile environments. Legacy wrapper configurations like conda provide isolation but rely on slow Python-based search loops that waste valuable sprint time.

uv is an ultra-fast Python package installer and resolver written in Rust. By compiling abstract constraint logic directly to native binaries and leveraging global content-addressable caches, uv performs dependency operations 10x to 100x faster than legacy alternatives.

Step-by-Step Production Initialization

# 1. Install the native uv binary engine into your local user space
curl -LsSf [https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh](https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh) | sh

# 2. Instantiate an isolated, project-specific metadata template folder
mkdir -p ndmc_modelling_project && cd ndmc_modelling_project
uv init

# 3. Compile an isolated target execution runtime directory (.venv)
uv venv

# 4. Bind your current shell session to the isolated environment
# On Linux / macOS systems:
source .venv/bin/activate
# On Windows PowerShell environments:
.venv\Scripts\activate

High-Throughput Package Aggregation with uv

When you add a dependency, uv updates your tracking configuration, resolves transitive prerequisites, and updates your environment state in a single step.

# Add fundamental mathematical libraries and data visualization layers
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn

# Inject interactive prototyping engines into the isolated project loop
uv add jupyter notebook ipykernel

# Force compile the exact DAG specification into a rigid, cross-platform lockfile
uv lock

# When a collaborator pulls your code, they reconstruct your environment identically using:
uv sync

The Immutable Rule of pyproject.toml

The configuration declared inside pyproject.toml serves as the explicit blueprint for your project. Never alter this metadata manually under time pressure. Always interact with it through uv add or uv remove commands to ensure your environment remains stable.

Module 3: R Environment Isolation via renv

The Mechanics of R Local Library Mapping

By default, R installs libraries globally within system directories like /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/ or C:\Program Files\R\. This global design can cause system-wide issues: upgrading a library for a new project can break old data processing pipelines that depend on older behavior.

renv solves this by modifying R’s internal search path (.libPaths()). It redirects package discovery to an isolated, project-specific folder structure, preventing version leaks between different projects.

# --- Run this inside your project's RStudio console session ---

# 1. Scaffold your local directory structure and override global .libPaths()
renv::init()

# 2. Install your required data processing and geospatial health modeling tools
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("data.table")
install.packages("sf")       # For geospatial health modeling
install.packages("brms")     # For Bayesian regression modeling

# 3. Interrogate your active session memory and serialize package states to renv.lock
renv::snapshot()

Team Sync Protocol for R Users

When a new team member clones the project, they avoid package conflicts by running:

# Read the renv.lock file to download and compile the exact versions required
renv::restore()

Module 4: Structural Topography (CCDS Directory Standard)

Theory of Folder Topography and Cognitive Load

In high-velocity collaborative analytics, directory layouts function as documentation. Arbitrary folder layouts force team members to spend time searching for scripts, features, and source datasets.

We enforce a strict adaptation of Cookiecutter Data Science (CCDS). This directory standard separates immutable inputs from intermediate transformations and production source files.

ndmc_modelling_project/
├── .venv/                  # Python isolated execution engine (NEVER track in version control)
├── renv/                   # R isolated project library assets (NEVER track in version control)
├── renv.lock               # Cryptographic blueprint file for R dependency states
├── pyproject.toml          # Declarative specification configuration for Python environments
├── uv.lock                 # Serialized, immutable Python graph layout lockfile
├── .gitignore              # Project gatekeeper file (prevents tracking caches and huge data payloads)
├── README.md               # Documentation: Architecture overview, setup steps, and insights
├── data/
│   ├── raw/                # Immutable source data (Read-Only; never modify these files)
│   └── processed/          # Derived feature matrices and processed data frames
├── notebooks/              # Workspace for exploratory analysis and rapid prototyping
│   ├── 01_eda_demographics.ipynb
│   └── 02_initial_plots.R
├── src/                    # Tested, production-ready modular application code
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── data_cleaner.py     # Pure data manipulation pipelines
│   └── model_engine.py     # Analytical processing and modeling logic
└── app.py                  # Final deployment file (Streamlit / Shiny app presentation portal)

Module 5: Theory of Distributed Version Control

Git as a Directed Acyclic Graph of Object States

Git does not track file deltas or changes line by line. Instead, Git models version control as a historical series of project-wide snapshots stored within a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

Each node in this graph represents a specific state of the project, identified by a cryptographic hash. The graph grows linearly or branches out as you add new commits.

Understanding the Internals

  • The Blob: A compressed snapshot of a file’s content, stored independently of its filename or path.
  • The Tree: A directory listing object that links filenames and paths to their underlying content blobs.
  • The Commit: A permanent metadata record that points to a specific root Tree. It documents who created the changes, when they were made, and references the parent commit node that came before it.

Local Git Initialization Mechanics

Before tracking code changes, configure your local environment options to ensure all commits are correctly attributed to you in the shared repository history.

# 1. Inject your identity metadata into the global configuration layer
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@ephi.gov.et"

# 2. Force the standard base branch naming protocol to default to main
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# 3. Output your current active configuration profiles to confirm settings
git config --list

Writing a Robust .gitignore Gatekeeper File

To prevent tracking multi-gigabyte data files, local environment folders, or temporary system caches, create a .gitignore file in your root folder:

# Local Virtual Environments
.venv/
bin/
include/
lib/
share/
renv/library/
renv/staging/

# Runtime Engine Caches and Intermediate Code Artifacts
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.Rhistory
.RData
.Ruserdata

# OS-Specific Hidden Metadata Files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Sensitive Input Channels (Never upload source datasets to GitHub)
data/
*.csv
*.xlsx
*.parquet
*.json

The High-Velocity Working-Staging-Repository Loop

To save your work in Git, you move changes through three distinct areas: the Working Directory, the Staging Area, and the Git Repository.

# 1. Review the state of your working directory files
git status

# 2. Move your tracked files into the staging area (index)
git add .gitignore pyproject.toml README.md

# 3. Create a permanent commit node in your local repository
git commit -m "chore: instantiate unified CCDS directory layout with secure gitignore"

# 4. View your project's commit history graph
git log --oneline

Notebook Sanitation Guidelines

Jupyter and R-Markdown notebooks save execution outputs directly into their underlying JSON metadata. Staging uncleaned notebooks can inflate repository sizes and cause messy diff histories. Always clear your notebook outputs before staging changes.

# Clear execution outputs before tracking notebooks in Git
jupyter nbconvert --clear-output --inplace notebooks/*.ipynb

Module 6: Remote Team Collaboration via GitHub

The Decentralized Coordination Architecture

When collaborating on code, teams use an asymmetric topography: each analyst maintains an independent local copy of the repository graph on their workstation, and they synchronize their histories through a shared, central repository hosted on GitHub.

  Local Workspace [Analyst A] ───► (Feature Branch) ───┐
                                                       ▼
                                             Remote Central Engine
                                             [GitHub Core Repository]
                                                       ▲
  Local Workspace [Analyst B] ───► (Feature Branch) ───┘

Connecting Your Local Repository to GitHub

One designated team member creates a blank project organization container on GitHub and links their local repository to the remote address:

# Map your local repository to your shared GitHub organization space
git remote add origin git@github.com:ndmc-davt/family_delta_modelling.git

# Verify your remote connection configurations
git remote -v

# Push your main branch to the remote repository
git push -u origin main

Fast Team Member Onboarding Workflow

All other team members skip local initialization. Instead, they clone the shared remote repository and set up their isolated environments instantly:

  • Python Devs
  • R Analysts
# Clone the complete project repository from GitHub
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/family_delta_modelling.git
cd family_delta_modelling

# Reconstruct the shared virtual environment instantly using uv
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv sync
# Clone the complete project repository from GitHub
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/family_delta_modelling.git
cd family_delta_modelling

# Launch an R session and rebuild the isolated package library
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"

Module 7: Isolated Feature Branching

Why Modifying main Directly Ruins Team Workflows

If multiple team members push commits directly to the main branch simultaneously, the repository’s history will break down, causing frequent push rejections and broken code states.

To maintain a stable, working product, development should follow a Feature-Branch Workflow. The main branch always contains clean, working code, while all active development happens on short-lived feature branches.

# Step 1: Switch to main and pull the latest team changes
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Step 2: Create and switch to a dedicated feature branch
git checkout -b feat/epidemic-sir-model

# Step 3: Implement your changes inside your modular files (e.g., src/model_engine.py)
# Model prototyping phase...

# Step 4: Stage and commit your changes locally
git add src/model_engine.py
git commit -m "feat: integrate core deterministic SIR differential equation system"

# Step 5: Push your feature branch to the remote repository
git push origin feat/epidemic-sir-model

Once pushed, go to GitHub, open a Pull Request (PR), and have a teammate review and approve your changes before merging them into main.

Module 8: Managing and Resolving Merge Conflicts

Anatomy of a Conflict Node

Merge conflicts occur when two branches modify the exact same line of code in the same file, or when one developer deletes a file that another developer is actively editing. Because Git cannot automatically determine which change takes priority, it pauses the merge process so you can manually resolve the choice.

Encountering a Collision Scenario

You attempt to merge the updated main branch into your local development branch, but a teammate has already merged a conflicting change to the remote repository:

git checkout feat/epidemic-sir-model
git merge main
Auto-merging src/data_cleaner.py
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/data_cleaner.py
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

Deciphering Git Conflict Markers

When a conflict occurs, Git writes clear structural markers directly into the affected file (src/data_cleaner.py) to highlight the differences between the competing versions:

<<<<<<< HEAD
def clean_demographics(df):
    # Your local modification written on your feature branch
    return df.dropna(subset=['age', 'gender'])
=======
def clean_demographics(df):
    # The change your teammate merged to main ahead of you
    return df.fill_na({'age': 0, 'gender': 'Unknown'})
>>>>>>> main

Step-by-Step Resolution Plan

  1. Coordinate: Talk to your teammate to compare changes and decide on the correct logical combination.
  2. Refine: Open the file in an editor, delete the conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>), and write the clean, combined code.
  3. Save and Push: Stage the resolved file and commit your changes to complete the merge:
git add src/data_cleaner.py
git commit -m "fix: resolve demographics data engineering merge conflict"
git push origin feat/epidemic-sir-model

Module 9: The Hackathon Reference Matrix

This reference guide maps everyday project workflows to the correct protocols for both Python and R environments.

Project Workflow Goal Python Command Pattern R Command Pattern
Initialize Isolated Environment Engine uv venv renv::init()
Install Project Dependencies uv add <package_name> install.packages("<package_name>")
Serialize Dependency States (Locking) uv lock renv::snapshot()
Synchronize Shared Team Environments uv sync renv::restore()
Execute Production Code Pipelines python src/model_engine.py Rscript src/model_engine.R
Launch Live App Presentations streamlit run app.py Rscript -e "shiny::runApp('app.py')"

Submission Success Checklist

Verify Your Repository State Before Presenting to the Judges

Good luck teams. Initialize your environments and start hacking! 🚀

```

EPHI / NDMC Hackathon 2026 — Advanced Tooling Theory & Practice
